rules: Domain layer (Steam types, VDF parsing, manifest diffing) must not import from infrastructure or application layers
Application layer (downgrade orchestration, workflow coordination) must not import from infrastructure layer directly
Infrastructure layer (filesystem I/O, DepotDownloader subprocess, Steam path detection, manifest locking) implements interfaces defined in the domain layer
Frontend (React) communicates with the Rust backend only through Tauri IPC commands — no direct filesystem access
Shared types between frontend and backend are defined in the Rust domain layer and mirrored via Tauri's type generation

versioning: Bump package.json and src-tauri/Cargo.toml versions together for all changes

implementation: Use strongly-typed Tauri IPC commands with serde for serialization
Prefer async Rust (tokio) for I/O-bound operations like subprocess calls and file operations
Use Result<T, E> with custom error types in Rust — propagate errors to the frontend as typed responses
Keep React components small and focused; lift state to hooks or context when shared
Parse VDF/ACF files with a dedicated parser module — do not use regex for structured data
Subprocess calls to DepotDownloader must be cancellable and report progress back to the frontend
review: Verify all filesystem paths are cross-platform compatible (Linux, macOS, Windows)
Check that Tauri commands handle errors gracefully and return user-friendly messages
Ensure no hardcoded paths — all Steam/game paths must be detected or configurable
Verify subprocess spawning has proper timeout and cancellation handling
Check that sensitive data (Steam credentials) is never logged or persisted insecurely
documentation: Document Steam-specific concepts (depots, manifests, ACF format) for contributors unfamiliar with Steam internals
Keep specs focused on the what and why — implementation details belong in code comments
Use diagrams for multi-step workflows like the downgrade process
delivery: Use conventional commit messages (feat:, fix:, chore:, docs:, refactor:)
Use conventional branch names (feat/, fix/, chore/, docs/, refactor/)
Use normal merges (no squash) to preserve commit history
PR titles should follow conventional commit format
triage: Classify by affected layer: frontend, backend, or full-stack
Scope features to a single step of the downgrade process when possible
Tag issues affecting cross-platform behavior with the relevant OS
